candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be turned into a genuine theorem-schema with explicit conditions and impossibility results, rather than a suggestive analogy? The central open problem is to specify a formal class of self-modeling control systems, define 'maximally complete, globally accurate, and decision-usable self-representation' in that class, and prove whether the claimed limit follows from recursion, resource bounds, feedback-induced self-modification, or some combination. Without this, it remains unclear which parts of the conjecture are load-bearing and which are metaphorical carryovers from Gödel.

What exactly is the tradeoff structure among completeness, accuracy, usability, and stability in internal self-models? The conjecture says no internal representation can optimize all of these at once, but it does not yet identify whether the limitation is logical, computational, dynamical, or control-theoretic. A strong new problem is to map the Pareto frontier: under what architectures can an agent gain more self-knowledge, what must be sacrificed, and are update cascades or context dependence necessary consequences or merely common design failures?

How should 'self' be individuated in a self-modeling system so that the limitation claim is nontrivial and not an artifact of shifting system boundaries? Since enlarging the self-model changes the modeled system, the conjecture depends heavily on where the agent, controller, model, memory, and environment are separated. An unresolved tension is whether the impossibility arises from genuine reflexive structure or from boundary choices; exploring this requires principled criteria for system closure, temporal identity, and levels of description across computational, neuroscientific, and systems-theoretic redescriptions.
